dossier.overview:
Compatibility is Pokémon Resort's promise that a Pokémon can travel through the hub without losing identity or legitimate progress. The public Compatibility ontology (#/ontology) tracks which generation routes pass that round-trip checklist; the game runtime enforces it through OpenHome-backed transfer, canonical OHPKM storage, and Save+Exit safety invariants.

A route is compatible when Game A → Resort → Game B → (play, level up, evolve, learn moves) → Resort → Game A yields the same Pokémon: immutable identity fields unchanged, mutable gameplay fields updated correctly, and no duplicate or vanished instances.

sections.title: Compatibility guide
sections.summary: What compatible means, how round trips work, and how the public ontology links to Resort + OpenHome.

blocks.body:
Compatible does not mean “every field from every generation survives verbatim in every save format.” It means the Pokémon that returns is recognizably the same individual, with legitimate updates applied and nothing important silently discarded.

Minimum round-trip path (directional routes on the ontology graph):

1. Import from origin generation (Game A) into Resort / OpenHome storage.
2. Export to a target generation (Game B) — may be the same generation (self route) or a different one (cross-gen).
3. Play in Game B: level up, learn moves, evolve, change held item, gain ribbons the target game can record, etc.
4. Return to Resort — OpenHome syncWithGameData merges mutable gameplay changes into the canonical OHPKM.
5. Export back to Game A.
6. Verify: still valid in PKHeX/OpenHome checks; immutable identity intact; mutable fields reflect Game B play; only one visible copy exists at each step.

Failure modes the project tracks publicly: wrong level after return, OT or PID drift, lost ribbons or moves that should have merged, duplicate slots (same Pokémon in Resort and a save), or dropped Pokémon (vanished from both).

blocks.html:
Field classExamplesRound-trip rule
Immutable identityOriginal PID/personality (where preserved), OT name, TID/SID, origin game, met data, encryption constant, fateful flag, home tracker / openhomeIdMust not change unless the product explicitly documents a controlled conversion (e.g. Gen 3 PID regeneration preserving nature/shiny/gender).
Mutable gameplayLevel, EXP, moves, PP, EVs/AVs, held item, form after evolution, ability slot after evolution, friendship, contest stats, markings, ribbons (gain-only merge), Pokerus when non-zero incomingGame B changes merge into OHPKM on return via syncWithGameData; lossy projections to older gens omit unsupported fields but canonical OHPKM retains them for restoration.
Target-only / lossy on exportRibbons from future gens, geography, tera data, modern side sectionsMay be hidden in Game B's byte format but must survive in OHPKM and reappear when projecting to a format that supports them.
Resort-owned (not game bytes)Memories, accessories, travel history, resort friendship, box UI layout keyed by pkridStored on Resort record; does not replace OHPKM as canonical Pokémon payload.

sequenceDiagram
    participant GA as Game A save
    participant OH as OpenHome OHPKM
    participant R as Resort pkrid + placement
    participant GB as Game B save
    GA->>OH: pull-to-home / startTracking
    OH->>R: link openhomeId to pkrid
    OH->>GB: push-to-game convertOhpkm
    Note over GB: Player levels up, learns moves
    GB->>OH: syncWithGameData on return
    OH->>GA: convertOhpkm back to Game A
    Note over GA: Same OT/PID identity, updated level/moves

blocks.body:
The Compatibility page (#/ontology) is the public test matrix. It is data-driven from public/data/compatibility.json and edited in Operations Desk → Compatibility.

Structure:

• generations[] — Gen I–IX nodes on the graph
• games[] — individual titles linked to a generation (box art, platform, year)
• routes[] — directional legs: from → to generation ids (81 routes: 9 self + 72 cross-gen)
• statuses — green (working), blue (needs tests), yellow (edge cases), red (not working), gray (untested)

Route naming: gen3-gen5 means Gen III → Gen V → Gen III (through Resort). Self routes (gen2-gen2) test same-generation round trips. Cross routes test visiting another generation in the middle.

Each route card carries summary, lastUpdated, coverage label, relatedBugs[], and a tests[] checklist. Status changes are evidence-based — flip to green only when the checklist passes.

Current public stance (conservative): most routes gray (untested); gen2-gen2 and gen7-gen7 marked red (known self round-trip failures); gen1-gen1 marked green.
Captured from #/ontology on the local Vite dev site — generation nodes, directional route arrows, and status colors (green / red / gray).
blocks.caption: Public compatibility graph
Focus mode narrows the graph to one generation's game library
Route status definitions — what each color means on the graph

blocks.body:
The ontology records test results; pokemon-resort/ implements movement.

Two identifiers, one Pokémon:

• pkrid — Resort local record id. Box placement, memories, accessories, travel history, resort friendship, UI cache.
• openhomeId — OpenHome canonical payload id. OHPKM bytes in mons_v2/<openhomeId>.ohpkm; generation conversion; syncWithGameData on return.

Placement state (replacing legacy mirror sessions for OpenHome flows):

• RESORT_BOX — visible in Resort storage
• IN_GAME_SAVE — projected into a game slot
• HOME_BANK — in OpenHome bank storage

Movement path (target architecture):

• Transfer System screen — in-memory UI moves while open; disk writes only on Save+Exit
• OpenHome resort bridge — pull-to-home, push-to-game, sync-save (tools/OpenHome/dist/resort-bridge/resortBridge.mjs)
• OpenHomeMovementOrchestrator / OpenHomeTransferCore — native Resort wiring
• PKHeX bridge — legacy read/projection; not the writer for OpenHome-backed slots

On save open or return, OpenHome loadIfTracked + syncWithGameData merges Game B mutable data into OHPKM before the next export.

blocks.caption: Identity boundary — pkrid vs openhomeId
blocks.body:
## Product Identity Boundary

- `pkrid` is the local Resort record id. Resort-owned features such as box placement, memories, accessories, travel history, jobs, resort friendship, and UI/cache data should key off `pkrid`.
- `openhomeId` is the canonical Pokemon payload/movement id. It identifies the OHPKM bytes that OpenHome uses for generation conversion, save writes, and `syncWithGameData` updates when a tracked Pokemon levels up, evolves, learns moves, gains ribbons, or otherwise changes in a game.

blocks.caption: Transfer System — Emerald save loaded (game_transfer_emerald.webp, game_transfer_emerald_2.webp)

blocks.caption: Transfer ticket screen

blocks.body:
Compatibility requires conservation laws, not just correct bytes in the happy path.

UI conservation guard (always on): every tool action must preserve the multiset of Pokémon identities across game panel slots, Resort panel slots, and held-move buffers. Duplicate or delete in memory → action aborts with error feedback.

Save+Exit staging: OpenHome writes go to a staged save path first. Real .sav is replaced only after verification passes. Fail closed — if uncertain, abort and leave the player's save untouched.

Pull-to-home rule: destination Home slot must be guaranteed empty (never rely on swap/displacement). After pull, re-import staged save and verify every source slot is empty — prevents duplicate visible copies.

Push-to-game rule: record IN_GAME_SAVE placement only on success; target slot overwrite rules must be explicit.

OpenHome-backed slots during PKHeX projection must emit preserve_box_slot so legacy write-back does not overwrite tracked Pokémon.

Only one player-visible instance at a time: if the Pokémon is in a game, it is not in a Resort box; if in Resort, not in a game.

blocks.caption: Core product promise
blocks.body:
## Core product promise

- **Never duplicate** a Pokémon (player-visible “copy exists in both places”).
- **Never delete** a Pokémon (player-visible “it vanished”).
- When something is uncertain, **fail closed**: abort Save+Exit and keep the real save file unchanged.

sections.title: Recording route evidence
sections.summary: How to move a route from gray to green on the public ontology.

blocks.body:
When you complete a round-trip test for a generation pair:

1. Run the full path with copied test saves (never the player's real save first).
2. Capture before/after PKHeX or OpenHome inspection notes: OT, PID, level, moves, ribbons, slot occupancy.
3. Confirm no duplicate slots and no vanish after Save+Exit.
4. In Operations Desk → Compatibility, select the route on the graph, set status, fill tests[] checklist items.
5. Link relatedBugs if failures are tracked (e.g. BUG-TRANSFER-GEN2-SELF).
6. Save compatibility.json and verify on #/ontology.

Self routes are the minimum bar for a generation. Cross-gen routes add conversion stress: lossy projection to Game B, mutable merge on return, re-projection to Game A must still yield a legal, same-individual Pokémon.

blocks.html:
Checklist step (typical cross-gen route)Verifies
Transfer origin generation into Resortpull-to-home, pkrid link, source slot cleared
Transfer from Resort to target generationpush-to-game, IN_GAME_SAVE placement
Play in target (level/move change)Mutable merge path exercised
Return route to origin generationsyncWithGameData + re-conversion
Record edge cases and save-data notesRibbons, forms, languages, shiny, nicknames

• docs/transfer_system/README.md — Transfer System ownership map
